action.content: Write a parody of 'ars poetica'. Do not include the word 'parody' throughout your response.
action.label: context

action.content:
I want to be a play that people come to see,
Not because it’s new or strange or weird,
But because it’s something they can recognize,
A mirror held up to their daily life.

I want to be a play that doesn’t preach,
That doesn’t tell them what to think or feel,
But lets them sit and watch and think and feel,
And maybe, just maybe, understand themselves.

I want to be a play that’s not too long,
Not too short, not too loud, not too quiet,
Just right, like a good conversation,
Where everyone has something to say,
And everyone listens.

I want to be a play that ends with a question,
Not an answer, not a conclusion,
But a question that lingers in the air,
That makes them think about their own lives,
About their own choices, their own regrets.

I want to be a play that’s not about me,
Not about my opinions, my biases, my agenda,
But about the people in the play,
About their struggles, their triumphs, their failures,
About what it means to be human.

I want to be a play that’s not perfect,
That has flaws, that has mistakes, that has moments of doubt,
Because that’s what life is like,
And that’s what makes it real,
And that’s what makes it worth watching.

I want to be a play that’s not just for entertainment,
But for something more,
Something that stays with you long after the curtain falls,
Something that changes you,
Something that makes you want to be a better person.

I want to be a play that’s not just words on a page,
But words that come alive,
That move you, that touch you, that make you feel,
That make you want to reach out and touch someone,
To tell them, “I see you, I hear you, I understand you.”

I want to be a play that’s not just for the moment,
But for the long haul,
A play that can be watched again and again,
And each time, you see something new,
Something different, something deeper,
Something that speaks to you in a new way.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the audience,
But for the actors too,
A play that challenges them, that pushes them,
That makes them grow, that makes them learn,
That makes them want to be better actors,
Better people.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the critics,
But for the everyday person,
The person who walks into the theater,
Who doesn’t know what to expect,
Who just wants to be entertained,
Who just wants to be moved.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the present,
But for the future,
A play that can be passed down,
That can be performed in different times,
In different places, by different people,
And still be the same play,
Still be the same story,
Still be the same truth.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the few,
But for the many,
A play that can be understood by anyone,
No matter their background, no matter their age,
No matter their language, no matter their culture,
A play that speaks to the universal human experience,
A play that says, “We’re all in this together.”

I want to be a play that’s not just for the theater,
But for the world,
A play that can be performed in a park,
In a school, in a prison, in a refugee camp,
In any place where people gather,
Where people need to be reminded of what it means to be human,
Where people need to be reminded of what it means to care.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the now,
But for the always,
A play that’s timeless, that’s eternal,
That’s as relevant today as it was yesterday,
As it will be tomorrow,
A play that’s a part of us,
A play that’s a part of who we are.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the eyes,
But for the heart,
A play that touches the soul,
That makes you feel something deep inside,
Something you can’t quite put into words,
Something that stays with you,
Something that changes you,
Something that makes you want to be a better person.

I want to be a play that’s not just for the audience,
But for the world,
A play that’s a gift,
A gift to the people who watch it,
A gift to the people who perform it,
A gift to the world,
A gift that says, “You’re not alone, you’re not forgotten, you’re not invisible.”

I want to be a play that’s not just for the moment,
But for the forever,
A play that’s a part of history,
A play that’s a part of culture,
A play that’s a part of the human story,
A play that’s a part of us,
A play that’s a part of who we are,
A play that’s a part of who we’ll always be.
action.label: response
